comments.text: I believe one can optimise the hard lower bound to $c_1=1/8$ without the asymptotic o(1) using a similar counting argument. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum number of convex subsets in a set X of n points. ES theorem says that there exists a integer $m$ such that any subset of $m$ points guarantees the existence of at least one convex $r$-gon. The total number of of all possible subsets of size $m$ subsets is given by $\binom{n}{m}$ accounting for overcounting, each unique convex $r$-gon is counted exactly\[ \binom{n-r}{m-r} \]times. Therefore,\[ f(n) \ge \frac{\binom{n}{m}}{\binom{n-r}{m-r}} \]Simplifying:\[ f(n) \ge \frac{\frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}}{\frac{m!}{r!(m-r)!}} = \frac{\binom{n}{r}}{\binom{m}{r}} \]Using $\frac{\binom{n}{r}}{\binom{m}{r}} >(\frac{n}{m})^{r}$ This gives\[ f(n) \ge \left(\frac{n}{m}\right)^r \]Substitute the E-Sz upper bound $m \le 4^r$,\[ f(n) \ge \left(\frac{n}{2^{2r}}\right)^r = \frac{n^r}{2^{2r^2}} \]To optimise this, we set $r = \frac{1}{4} \log_2 n$ which gives\[ f(n) \ge 2^{\frac{1}{8} (\log_2 n)^2} \]

This may be a standard observation, but I do not see it in the remarks. It gives an explicit lower constant using the present best form of the Erdős--Szekeres theorem and a short entropy/double-counting argument. I write all logarithms below in base $2$. By Suk's theorem, $ES(k)=2^{k+o(k)}$. Equivalently, every $T$-point set in general position contains a convex subset of size $$ r=(1-o(1))\log_2 T. $$ Let $P$ be an arbitrary set of $n$ points in the plane, with no three collinear. Choose uniformly a $T$-subset $R\subset P$, where $$ T=\lfloor n^\alpha\rfloor,\qquad 0<\alpha<1. $$ For each $R$, choose one convex $r$-subset $C(R)\subset R$. In entropy form, $$ H(C(R))\ge H(R)-H(R\mid C(R)). $$ Since $$ H(R)=\log_2 {n\choose T} $$ and, after $C(R)$ is known, there are at most ${n-r\choose T-r}$ possible choices for $R$, we get $$ H(C(R))\ge \log_2 {n\choose T}-\log_2 {n-r\choose T-r}. $$ Thus, if $N_r(P)$ denotes the number of convex $r$-subsets of $P$, then $$ \log_2 N_r(P)\ge \log_2 {n\choose T}-\log_2 {n-r\choose T-r}, $$ or equivalently $$ N_r(P)\ge { {n\choose T}\over {n-r\choose T-r}} = { {n\choose r}\over {T\choose r}}. $$ Therefore $$ \log_2 N_r(P) \ge r\log_2(n/T)-o((\log n)^2). $$ Since $T=n^\alpha+O(1)$ and $r=(\alpha-o(1))\log_2 n$, this gives $$ \log_2 N_r(P) \ge (\alpha(1-\alpha)-o(1))(\log_2 n)^2. $$ Optimizing at $\alpha=1/2$, every $n$-point set in general position determines at least $$ 2^{(1/4-o(1))(\log_2 n)^2} $$ convex subsets. Hence $$ f(n)\ge 2^{(1/4-o(1))(\log_2 n)^2}, $$ and so $$ \liminf_{n\to\infty} {\log_2 f(n)\over (\log_2 n)^2} \ge {1\over 4}. $$ For comparison, the classical Erdős--Szekeres construction with $2^{k-2}$ points and no $k$ points in convex position gives, by taking $k=\lceil \log_2 n\rceil+O(1)$, $$ f(n)\le \sum_{j<k}{n\choose j} \le \left({en\over k}\right)^k = 2^{(1+o(1))(\log_2 n)^2}. $$ Thus the current simple bounds, with base-$2$ logarithms, are $$ {1\over 4} \le \liminf_{n\to\infty} {\log_2 f(n)\over(\log_2 n)^2} \le \limsup_{n\to\infty} {\log_2 f(n)\over(\log_2 n)^2} \le 1. $$ With natural logarithms this becomes $$ {1\over 4\log 2} \le \liminf_{n\to\infty} {\log f(n)\over(\log n)^2} \le \limsup_{n\to\infty} {\log f(n)\over(\log n)^2} \le {1\over \log 2}. $$
Thanks. Standard check believed this to be correct.
